en.extract:
The olive warbler (Peucedramus taeniatus) is a small passerine bird. It is the only member of the genus Peucedramus and the family Peucedramidae.
This species breeds from southern Arizona and New Mexico, USA, south through Mexico to Nicaragua. It is the only bird family endemic to North America (including Central America). It was in the past classed with the Parulidae (New World warblers), but DNA studies suggest that it split early from the other related passerines, prior to the differentiation of the entire New World warbler/American sparrow/Icterid group. It is therefore now given a family of its own.
It is an insectivorous species of coniferous forests. Though it is often said to be non-migratory, most New Mexican birds leave the state from November to late February. It lays 3–4 eggs in a tree nest.


Taxonomy
The olive warbler was originally placed in the New World warbler (family Parulidae) genus Dendroica, a group which it closely resembles, particularly in having nine primaries and similar skin. In spite of being assigned to its own genus in 1875, its affinities were a source of contention. The shape of the basihyal bone in the skull, and aspects of its behaviour led to the suggestion that it was instead an Old World warbler in the family Sylviidae. That it was not in the family Parulidae was supported by the arrangement of muscles in the legs. DNA–DNA hybridization placed the olive warbler as an early branch of the finch clade (which included the finches, cardinals and Hawaiian honeycreepers) and the New World sparrow clade (which includes the tanagers, icterids and New World warblers), and a 1998 study of mitochondrial DNA confirmed its status as being far removed from the New World warblers.
The generic name of the olive warbler, Peucedramus, is derived from the Greek peuke for a fir tree and dromos for runner, (from trekho, meaning run), a reference to its feeding habitat and behaviour. The species name, taeniatus, is from the Latin taenia for a headband, and atus for possessing, a reference to its facial markings. The original specific name for the species was olivaceus; however that name was preoccupied and the name was changed.


Description
The olive warbler is a medium-sized warbler, 13 to 14 cm (5.1–5.5 in) in length and weighing 9.5 to 12 g (0.34–0.42 oz). It shows clinal variation in size, with more northern populations being larger than southern ones, a phenomenon known as Bergmann's rule. The olive warbler is a long-winged bird. The plumage of the male is mostly grey body with some olive-green on the wings and two white wing bars. The male's head and breast are "tawny-orange", and there is a black patch through the eye. In the female and juvenile, the orange is replaced by yellow, and the black mask is more diffuse. In addition to differences in size, plumage varies geographically as well, with southern birds having more brightly coloured plumage.
The song consists of clear whistles rendered as hirrJI hirrJI hirrJI, plida plida plida chir chir, etc. The male sings throughout the year, with the frequency of the singing increasing in late winter and reaching a peak in early spring. During the year the male sings the most during the midmorning, but during spring the male sings constantly during mornings and late afternoon. Song is usually delivered from the canopy or other tall trees.


Distribution and habitat

The olive warbler is distributed from the southwestern United States to Nicaragua, making it the only bird family endemic to North America. In the northern part of its range it has a continuous distribution from Yavapai County, Arizona and the southwestern tip of New Mexico along central Mexico to southern Mexico. It has a disjunct distribution across the rest of its range, with populations in Tamaulipas, Coahuila and Sonora and Chihuahua in Mexico, and further south in southwest Guatemala and from northern El Salvador and central Honduras to northern Nicaragua.
The olive warbler is a bird of mountains and highlands. In northern areas of its range it occurs from 2,600 m (8,500 ft) above sea level or more, in some parts of its range it may not occur below 3,500 m (11,500 ft). In Oaxaca in Mexico it occupies a range of between 1,700–3,000 m (5,600–9,800 ft) further south in Guatemala it ranges down to 1,850 m (6,070 ft) and at the southern end of its range it can be found as low as 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in Honduras and in Nicaragua it occupies a belt of forest from 1,070–1,370 m (3,510–4,490 ft) . They typically inhabit conifer forests, such as ponderosa and sugar pine forests in Arizona, Abies common fir forests, oak, and pine forests in central Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras. These forests range from humid to semi-arid. In coastal regions stands of palms may be used, and in the Valley of Mexico pine and alder forests are inhabited.
Over most of its range the species is resident, but there is evidence that the most northerly populations are partial migrants. Birds in Arizona, New Mexico, and Mexico apparently move away from their breeding grounds, although what exact movements are made is unclear. It has been suggested that they are either "down slope migrants", moving to lower elevations, or dispersive, as some records show their presence in Texas. Birds attributed to the northern race P. t. arizonae have been recorded near Tepic, in Nayarit. Nevertheless, some birds remain in the northern areas of their range year round.


Diet and feeding
The olive warbler is an insectivore, taking insects and other arthropods. No specific information exists about the actual prey species taken, except that they will take the larvae of Tortricidae moths. It forages in forests in the canopy and subcanopy. In feeds in the outer branches and twigs. It mostly feeds in the branches of ponderosa pines, but also feeds in the branches of grey and silverleaf oaks.


References


Further reading
Ewing, K. E. (1971). "Olive Warbler at Mccoy Eagle County Colorado". Colorado Field Ornithologist. 9 (32).
Hobart, H. H. (1991). Comparative karyology in nine-primaried oscines (Aves) (Ph.D. thesis). The University of Arizona.
Lovette, I. J.; Hochachka, W. M. (2006). "Simultaneous effects of phylogenetic niche conservatism and competition on avian community structure". Ecology. 87 (7. Suppl): S14–S28. doi:10.1890/0012-9658(2006)87[14:SEOPNC]2.0.CO;2. PMID 16922299.
Martinez-Morales, Miguel Angel (2004). "New records of birds in the cloud forest of northeastern Hidalgo, Mexico" (PDF). Huitzil (in Spanish and English). 5 (2): 12–19. Archived from the original (PDF) on 13 September 2004.
Rusterholz, K. A. (1981). "Competition and the Structure of an Avian Foraging Guild". American Naturalist. 118 (2): 173–190. doi:10.1086/283813. S2CID 85002007.

The '''olive warbler''' ('''''Peucedramus taeniatus''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]]. It is the only member of the genus '''''Peucedramus''''' and the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] '''Peucedramidae'''.

This species breeds from southern [[Arizona]] and [[New Mexico]],<ref name=NatureServe/> USA, south through Mexico to Nicaragua. It is the only bird family endemic to North America (including Central America).<ref>{{cite book|last1=Wheatley|first1=Nigel|last2=Brewer|first2=David|title=Where to Watch Birds in Central America, Mexico, and the Caribbean |isbn=0-691-09515-9|publisher=Princeton University Press|location= Princeton, New Jersey|year= 2001}}</ref> It was in the past classed with the Parulidae ([[New World warbler]]s), but DNA studies suggest that it split early from the other related passerines, prior to the differentiation of the entire New World warbler/[[American sparrow]]/[[Icterid]] group. It is therefore now given a family of its own.

It is an insectivorous species of coniferous forests. Though it is often said to be [[bird migration|non-migratory]],<ref name=Sibley/> most New Mexican birds leave the state from November to late February.<ref>{{cite book | last = Parmeter | first = John |author2=Neville, Bruce |author3=Emkalns, Doug | year = 2002 | title = New Mexico Bird Finding Guide | edition = 3rd | pages = 314–315 | publisher = New Mexico Ornithological Society}}</ref> It lays 3–4 eggs in a tree nest.

==Taxonomy==
The olive warbler was originally placed in the New World warbler (family Parulidae) genus ''[[Dendroica]]'', a group which it closely resembles, particularly in having nine [[flight feather|primaries]] and similar skin. In spite of being assigned to its own genus in 1875, its affinities were a source of contention.<ref name = "Webster"/> The shape of the basihyal bone in the skull, and aspects of its behaviour led to the suggestion that it was instead an [[Old World warbler]] in the family Sylviidae. That it was not in the family Parulidae was supported by the arrangement of muscles in the legs. [[DNA–DNA hybridization]] placed the olive warbler as an early branch of the [[finch]] clade (which included the finches, cardinals and [[Hawaiian honeycreeper]]s) and the New World sparrow clade (which includes the [[tanager]]s, [[icterid]]s and New World warblers),<ref>{{cite book|last1=Sibley|first1=C. G.|first2=B. L. | last2 = Monroe Jr|title=Distribution and taxonomy of birds of the world|publisher=Yale University Press|location=New Haven, Connecticut|year=1990|isbn=0-300-05549-8}}</ref> and a 1998 study of [[mitochondrial DNA]] confirmed its status as being far removed from the New World warblers.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Groth|first=Jeff|title=Molecular Phylogenetics of Finches and Sparrows: Consequences of Character State Removal in Cytochrome b Sequences|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=10|issue=3|pages=377–390 | doi = 10.1006/mpev.1998.0540|pmid=10051390|year=1998}}</ref>

The [[genus|generic]] name of the olive warbler, ''Peucedramus'', is derived from the [[Ancient Greek|Greek]] ''peuke'' for a fir tree and ''dromos'' for runner, (from ''trekho'', meaning run), a reference to its feeding habitat and behaviour. The species name, ''taeniatus'', is from the [[Latin]] ''taenia'' for a headband, and ''atus'' for possessing, a reference to its facial markings.<ref>{{cite book | last = Jobling | first = James A. | title = A Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher = [[Oxford University Press]] | year = 1991 | location = Oxford | page = 179 | isbn = 0-19-854634-3}}</ref> The original specific name for the species was ''olivaceus''; however that name was [[International Code of Zoological Nomenclature|preoccupied]] and the name was changed.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Zimmer|first=J.T. |year=1948|title=The specific name of the Olive Warbler|journal=Auk|volume=65|issue=1|pages=126–127|url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/auk/vol65/iss1/20/|doi=10.2307/4080235|jstor=4080235 }}</ref>

==Description==
The olive warbler is a medium-sized [[warbler]], {{cvt|13|to(-)|14|cm|in}} in length and weighing {{cvt|9.5|to(-)|12|g|oz}}. It shows clinal variation in size, with more northern populations being larger than southern ones, a phenomenon known as [[Bergmann's rule]].<ref name ="Webster">{{cite journal|last=Webster|year=1962|title=Systematic and ecologic notes on the Olive Warbler|journal=Wilson Bulletin|volume=74 |pages=417–425|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v074n04/p0417-p0425.pdf}}</ref> The olive warbler is a long-winged bird. The [[plumage]] of the male is mostly grey body with some olive-green on the wings and two white wing bars. The male's head and breast are "tawny-orange",<ref name=Sibley>{{cite book | author=Sibley, David | title=[[The Sibley Guide to Birds]] | publisher=Knopf | year=2000 | page=[https://archive.org/details/sibleyguidetobir00sibl_0/page/458 458] | isbn=0-679-45122-6 | author-link=David Allen Sibley }}</ref> and there is a black patch through the eye. In the female and juvenile, the orange is replaced by yellow, and the black mask is more diffuse. In addition to differences in size, plumage varies geographically as well, with southern birds having more brightly coloured plumage.

The [[bird vocalization|song]] consists of clear whistles rendered as ''hirrJI hirrJI hirrJI'', ''plida plida plida chir chir'', etc.<ref name=Sibley/> The male sings throughout the year, with the frequency of the singing increasing in late winter and reaching a peak in early spring. During the year the male sings the most during the midmorning, but during spring the male sings constantly during mornings and late afternoon. Song is usually delivered from the canopy or other tall trees.<ref name = "BNA"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:PeucedramusCycle-en.png|thumb|left|Annual cycle]]
The olive warbler is distributed from the southwestern United States to [[Nicaragua]], making it the only bird [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[endemism|endemic]] to North America.<ref name = "BNA"/> In the northern part of its range it has a continuous distribution from [[Yavapai County, Arizona]] and the southwestern tip of [[New Mexico]] along central Mexico to southern Mexico. It has a [[disjunct distribution]] across the rest of its range, with populations in [[Tamaulipas]], [[Coahuila]] and [[Sonora]] and [[Chihuahua (state)|Chihuahua]] in Mexico, and further south in southwest [[Guatemala]] and from northern [[El Salvador]] and central [[Honduras]] to northern Nicaragua.<ref name = "BNA">{{cite journal|url=https://bna.birds.cornell.edu/bna/species/310|title=Olive Warbler (''Peucedramus taeniatus'')|last=Lowther|first=Peter E|author2=Jorge Nocedal|year=1997|journal=Birds of North America Online|publisher=Ithaca: Cornell Lab of Ornithology|access-date=1 May 2009 | doi = 10.2173/bna.310|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

The olive warbler is a bird of mountains and highlands. In northern areas of its range it occurs from {{cvt|2600|m|ft}} above sea level or more, in some parts of its range it may not occur below {{cvt|3500|m|ft}}. In [[Oaxaca]] in Mexico it occupies a range of between {{cvt|1700|–|3000|m|ft}} further south in Guatemala it ranges down to {{cvt|1850|m|ft}} and at the southern end of its range it can be found as low as {{cvt|1000|m|ft}} in Honduras and in Nicaragua it occupies a belt of forest from {{cvt|1070|–|1370|m|ft}} .<ref name = "BNA"/> They typically inhabit conifer forests, such as [[Pinus ponderosa|ponderosa]] and [[Pinus lambertiana|sugar]] pine forests in Arizona, ''[[Abies]]'' common fir forests, [[oak]], and pine forests in central Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras. These forests range from humid to semi-arid.<ref name ="Webster"/> In coastal regions stands of palms may be used, and in the [[Valley of Mexico]] pine and alder forests are inhabited.<ref name = "BNA"/>

Over most of its range the species is {{birdgloss|resident}}, but there is evidence that the most northerly populations are [[bird migration|partial migrants]]. Birds in Arizona, New Mexico, and Mexico apparently move away from their breeding grounds, although what exact movements are made is unclear. It has been suggested that they are either "down slope migrants", moving to lower elevations, or dispersive, as some records show their presence in [[Texas]]. Birds attributed to the northern race ''P. t. arizonae'' have been recorded near [[Tepic]], in [[Nayarit]]. Nevertheless, some birds remain in the northern areas of their range year round.<ref name = "BNA"/><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Vander Wall|author2=Stephen B. |author3=Sullivan, Kelly   |name-list-style=amp |year=1977|title=Olive Warblers in the San Francisco Mountains, Arizona|journal=Western Birds|volume=8|issue=3|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wb/v08n03/p0106-p0106.pdf}}</ref>

==Diet and feeding==
The olive warbler is an insectivore, taking insects and other arthropods. No specific information exists about the actual prey species taken, except that they will take the larvae of [[Tortricidae]] moths.<ref name="BNA" /> It forages in forests in the canopy and subcanopy. In feeds in the outer branches and twigs.<ref name="hbwfamily">{{cite journal | last1=Cursonl | first1=J |last2=Bonan | first2=A | editor-first1=Shawn M. | editor-first2=Brooke K. | editor-first3=Paul G. | editor-first4=Thomas S. | editor-last1=Billerman | editor-last2=Keeney | editor-last3=Rodewald | editor-last4=Schulenberg |year=2017 |title= Olive Warbler (Peucedramidae) |journal=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=http://www.hbw.com/family/olive-warbler-peucedramidae|publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona | doi=10.2173/bow.peuced1.01 | s2cid=216495034 |access-date=13 December 2016 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> It mostly feeds in the branches of ponderosa pines, but also feeds in the branches of [[Quercus grisea|grey]] and [[Quercus hypoleucoides|silverleaf oaks]].<ref name="BNA" />

==References==

M.|year=2006|title=Simultaneous effects of phylogenetic niche conservatism and competition on avian community structure|journal=Ecology|volume=87|issue= 7. Suppl|pages= S14–S28|pmid=16922299|doi=10.1890/0012-9658(2006)87[14:SEOPNC]2.0.CO;2|doi-access=free}}
*{{cite journal|last=Martinez-Morales|first=Miguel Angel|year=2004|title=New records of birds in the cloud forest of northeastern Hidalgo, Mexico|journal=Huitzil|volume=5|issue=2|pages=12–19|language=Spanish, English|url= http://www.huitzil.net/nr-23.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040913060050/http://www.huitzil.net/nr-23.pdf|archive-date=13 September 2004}}

zh.extract:
橄榄绿森莺（台湾作橄綠森鶯，学名：Peucedramus taeniatus），是雀形目绿森莺科的唯一一种鸟类。
橄榄绿森莺体重约11克，翼长约71毫米，嘴峰长约14.3毫米，喙宽度约3.1毫米，喙厚度约2.9毫米，跗蹠长约18.6毫米，尾长约52.8毫米。橄榄绿森莺是部分迁徙的鸟类，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
P. t. arizonae：分布于美国西南部山区至墨西哥北部（奇瓦瓦州北部和科阿韦拉州北部）。
P. t. jaliscensis：分布于墨西哥西北部山区（奇瓦瓦州南部至哈利斯科州西南部和科利马州）。
P. t. giraudi：分布于墨西哥中部山区（哈利斯科州至普埃布拉州北部和韦拉克鲁斯州中西部）。
P. t. taeniatus：分布于墨西哥南部山区（格雷罗、瓦哈卡、恰帕斯）至危地马拉西部地区。
P. t. micrus：分布于萨尔瓦多、洪都拉斯和尼加拉瓜北部的山区。
